explanation: The property states that globally, whenever the inspiratory airway pressure at the patient is exceeded (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp), then in the very next state (X), the inspiratory phase must end (inspiratoryPhaseEnd), the expiratory phase must begin (expiratoryPhaseStart), the inspiratory valve must close (inValveClose), and the expiratory valve must open (outValveOpen). This captures the immediate reaction of truncating inspiration and transitioning to expiration by adjusting the valves to relieve pressure, which is characteristic of the fail-safe response to such a high-pressure alarm condition.

The property states that globally, whenever the inspiratory airway pressure at the patient is exceeded (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp), then in the very next state, both the inspiratory phase ends (inspiratoryPhaseEnd) and the expiratory phase begins (expiratoryPhaseStart). This captures the requirement for the system to immediately react to the pressure exceedance by truncating the inspiratory phase and transitioning to expiration.

The property states that globally, whenever the inspiratory airway pressure at the patient is exceeded (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp), then in the very next state, the inspiratory phase must end, the expiratory phase must begin, the inspiratory valve must close, and the expiratory valve must open. This captures the system's immediate reaction to the pressure being exceeded by truncating the inspiratory phase and quickly relieving pressure through expiration.

The property states that globally, whenever the inspiratory airway pressure at the patient is exceeded, the system must immediately (in the next state) respond by ending the inspiratory phase, starting the expiratory phase, and opening the out valve to relieve pressure. This directly translates the trigger condition (pressure exceeded) to the specified immediate consequences (truncating inspiratory phase, transitioning to expiration, relieving pressure).

The property states that globally, if the inspiratory airway pressure at the patient is exceeded (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp), then in the very next state, the system shall immediately transition to the expiratory phase (expiratoryPhase). This directly captures the requirement for the system to react by truncating the inspiratory phase and transitioning to expiration, which quickly relieves the pressure.

The property states that globally, if the inspiratory airway pressure at the patient is exceeded (Max P insp alarm), then in the very next state, the inspiratory phase must end and the expiratory phase must immediately begin. This captures the 'react to ... by truncating the inspiratory phase and immediately transitioning to expiration' requirement.

The property states that globally, whenever the inspiratory airway pressure at the patient is exceeded, it must be followed in the very next state by the end of the inspiratory phase and the immediate start of the expiratory phase. This captures the requirement for the system to react to the pressure exceedance by immediately truncating the inspiratory phase and transitioning to expiration.

The property states that globally, whenever the inspiratory airway pressure at the patient is exceeded (Max P insp alarm SAV.4), the inspiratory phase shall immediately end and the expiratory phase shall immediately begin in the very next state, capturing the requirement for immediate transition to expiration and quick pressure relief.

The LTL property states that globally (G), if the inspiratory airway pressure at the patient is exceeded (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp), then in the very next state (X), both the inspiratory phase must end (inspiratoryPhaseEnd) and the expiratory phase must begin (expiratoryPhaseStart). This captures the immediate reaction of truncating the inspiratory phase and transitioning to expiration upon the pressure being exceeded.

The property states that globally, whenever the inspiratory airway pressure at the patient is exceeded (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp), then in the very next state, the inspiratory phase must end (inspiratoryPhaseEnd), the expiratory phase must begin (expiratoryPhaseStart), the inspiratory valve must close (inValveClose), and the expiratory valve must open (outValveOpen). This accurately captures the requirement for the system to immediately truncate the inspiratory phase and transition to expiration to relieve pressure upon the alarm condition.
